text: Digital environments are structurally hostile to flow, and the reasons are precise. Flow requires a single, clear goal; social media offers an infinite, shifting stream of micro-goals — likes, notifications, new posts — that fragment attention into incompletable loops. Flow requires challenge calibrated to skill; algorithmic feeds are calibrated to arousal, not growth. Flow requires uninterrupted time; push notifications are architecturally designed to interrupt at intervals short enough to prevent deep absorption. Psychologist Adam Alter, in Irresistible, calls these "variable reward schedules" — borrowed from slot machine design. The result is an environment that mimics the surface features of engagement while systematically blocking the conditions that make genuine flow possible.
question: According to the passage, how do algorithmic feeds differ from the challenge calibration that flow requires?

options.text: Algorithmic feeds are calibrated to arousal rather than to skill growth
Algorithmic feeds present challenges that are consistently too easy for skilled users
Algorithmic feeds require multitasking, which disrupts single-task focus
Algorithmic feeds suppress dopamine release, which flow depends on
